title: Exposing Tools to Agents
concept: Describing tool schemas with names and descriptions so the agent understands when and how to call them.

exercises.prompt: Quick start! What is an AI agent?

options.text: An AI that can take actions and use tools to reach a goal
A person who sells AI software
A drawing made by an image generator

exercises.explain: An AI agent doesn't just chat back. It plans, decides, and uses tools (like search or a calculator) to actually get things done toward a goal.

cards.title: How does the agent know what it can do?
cards.body: An agent can't use a tool it doesn't know exists. Before it can call a tool, someone has to hand it a clear menu of options.

cards.title: Tool schema
cards.body: A tool schema is a short label card for a tool. It gives the tool a name and a description so the agent knows when and how to use it.

cards.title: Like a restaurant menu
cards.body: A menu lists each dish with a name and a description so you can order the right thing. Tool schemas are the agent's menu of actions it can 'order'.

cards.title: A weather tool
cards.body: Name: get_weather. Description: 'Returns today's temperature for a given city.' Now the agent knows to call this tool when someone asks about the weather.

cards.title: Myth: agents just 'know' every tool
cards.body: Nope! An agent only knows about tools you describe to it. A vague or missing description means the agent may never pick the right tool.

cards.title: Clear vs. vague schema
cards.body: The clearer the name and description, the easier it is for the agent to choose correctly.

left.text: get_weather: 'Returns today's temperature for a city.'

exercises.prompt: What is the main job of a tool schema?

options.text: To tell the agent a tool's name and when/how to use it
To make the agent respond faster
To hide tools from the agent
To translate the agent into another language

exercises.explain: A tool schema describes a tool with a clear name and description, so the agent can decide when and how to call it.

exercises.prompt: True or False: An agent can use a tool even if the tool was never described to it.
exercises.explain: False. An agent can only choose from tools it has been given schemas for. No description means the tool is invisible to the agent.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the blank about tool schemas.
exercises.sentence: A good tool schema gives each tool a clear name and a helpful description.
exercises.explain: The description tells the agent what the tool does and when to use it, which is just as important as the name.

exercises.prompt: Which tool description was written by an AI to be clear for an agent?

options.text: send_email: 'Sends an email to a given address with a subject and message body.'

exercises.explain: The first clearly states what the tool does and what inputs it needs. Clear, specific descriptions help the agent pick the right tool at the right time.

exercises.prompt: An agent has two tools: 'get_weather' (returns a city's temperature) and 'send_email' (sends an email). A user asks, 'What's the temperature in Tokyo?' Why will the agent pick get_weather?

options.text: Because its name and description match the request about temperature in a city
Because it is listed first in the code
Because the agent picks a random tool each time

exercises.explain: Agents match a request to the tool whose schema fits best. Because get_weather's clear name and description mention temperature for a city, the agent knows it's the right choice. That's why writing good names and descriptions is the key to exposing tools to agents.
